summary.difficultyAssessment: Distribution is D1:81(37.2%) D2:88(40.4%) D3:49(22.5%). D2 is spot-on at ~40%. D1 is overweight by ~7% and D3 is underweight by ~7.5% compared to the target 30:40:30 split. The D3 questions that do exist are genuinely challenging multi-step problems. The shortfall in D3 means high-ability children get fewer stretch questions. Recommend converting 10-15 D1 questions to D3 difficulty.
summary.coverageAssessment: All 8 GL question types from the research are covered: place value identification (Q159-166), ordering/comparing (Q1,9,15,40,48,56,67,79,86,100,113,116,128,145), fraction-decimal-percentage conversion (Q8,18,22,30,43,54,69,74,90,94,107,124,140,167-174,189-194,200-204), decimal arithmetic (Q2,3,7,12,16,19,25,28,32,36,37,44,46,52,55,61,68,71,76,82,89,95,98,106,108,121,125,129,132,182-188,195-199,209-212,216-218), multiply/divide by 10/100/1000 (Q5,6,11,23,29,34,38,39,45,53,58,66,75,81,87,93,105,114,115,120,130,175-181,205-208), rounding (Q13,24,50,60,73,84,96,109,122,133,213-216), word problems with decimals (Q136,138,141,144,146,150,154,155,157,158,185,199), and dividing decimal by decimal (Q27,35,72,88,102,112,131,139,143). Number line/positioning is the only type not explicitly tested as a standalone question, though comparison questions cover this skill. Strong real-world context variety: money, measurement, science, sport.
summary.teachingEfficacyAssessment: 14 sub-concepts with 28 lessons covering the core skills well. Lesson quality is generally high with good use of PlaceValueChart and WorkedExample visual components. Key concern: multiple sub-concepts show '[No lesson found]' in the mapping data, meaning the teach screen content was not extracted — this includes comparing-decimals, master-column-addition-decimals, subtracting-decimals, rounding-decimals, and dividing-to-decimal. The lessons DO exist for most of these (verified in lesson data), so this appears to be an extraction issue rather than a content gap. However, master-column-addition-decimals has NO lessons AND no learning goals in the mapping data, which is a genuine gap — addition of decimals is only covered implicitly via subtraction lessons. One duplicate option found in a lesson interaction (decimal-place-value-keyfact interact screen has '6' listed twice).
topPriorities: 1. Increase D3 question count — currently 22.5% vs target 30%. Add 16+ harder multi-step questions combining 3+ skills (ordering mixed forms, multi-step word problems with context interpretation, missing number problems)
2. Add explicit lessons for master-column-addition-decimals — this is a core skill with ~20 questions mapped to it but zero lessons
3. Fix the 3 questions with poor/duplicate distractor design (Q5 has 6 and 6.0 as separate options, Q49 has too-tight spacing making it pure guessing, lesson interaction has duplicate '6' option)

issues.title: D3 questions significantly underweight (22.5% vs target 30%)
issues.description: The difficulty distribution is D1:81(37.2%) D2:88(40.4%) D3:49(22.5%). D1 is 7% overweight and D3 is 7.5% underweight compared to the target 30:40:30 split. This means children who need stretching at the harder end get fewer practice opportunities. The D3 questions that exist are well-designed multi-step problems, but there are not enough of them.
issues.suggestedFix: Convert 10-15 easier D1 questions to D3 difficulty by adding multi-step reasoning, mixed forms (fractions/decimals/percentages), context interpretation, or missing-number algebra. Good candidates for upgrade: simple addition/subtraction questions where carrying is straightforward (e.g. Q2, Q3, Q36, Q44, Q61, Q76, Q95, Q108, Q125) could be made harder by adding a second step or requiring conversion first.

issues.title: No standalone number line / positioning questions
issues.description: GL research identifies 'Number Line / Positioning' as a distinct question type (Type 8) that appears on papers. The current question bank has no questions asking children to identify a decimal value from a number line diagram. Comparison questions partially cover this skill but don't test visual number line reading.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 5-8 number line questions across difficulties: D1 (number line marked in tenths, identify value), D2 (number line with only endpoints, identify unmarked value), D3 (estimate a position between marks). These would need SVG diagrams.

issues.title: No lessons for master-column-addition-decimals sub-concept
issues.description: The sub-concept 'master-column-addition-decimals' has approximately 20 questions mapped to it (Q2, Q12, Q16, Q25, Q28, Q36, Q44, Q52, Q61, Q71, Q76, Q89, Q95, Q98, Q108, Q125, Q132, etc.) but the mapping data shows '[No lesson found]' with empty learning goals. Unlike other '[No lesson found]' entries where lessons exist but weren't extracted, this sub-concept genuinely appears to lack lesson content. Adding decimals with carrying across the decimal point is a core GL skill.
issues.suggestedFix: Create 2 lessons for this sub-concept: (1) a step-by-step lesson teaching column addition of decimals with emphasis on lining up decimal points and carrying, (2) a spot-the-mistake lesson addressing the misaligned column error (adding 3.4 + 0.56 by right-aligning as 3.4 + 5.6).

issues.title: Options include both '6' and '6.0' which are mathematically equivalent
issues.description: Q5 asks 'A bag of sweets costs £0.60. What do 10 bags cost?' Options are: 0.06, 6, 60, 0.6, 6.0. The correct answer is '6' (index 1). However option '6.0' (index 4) is mathematically identical to '6'. A child who correctly understands the problem could reasonably select either. This creates ambiguity.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace '6.0' with a different distractor that exploits a specific misconception. For example: '0.006' (dividing by 10 instead of multiplying) or '600' (multiplying by 1000 instead of 10).

issues.title: D1 rating too easy for dividing by 100 with a decimal resulting in thousandths
issues.description: Q29: 'A school raises £7.50 and shares it equally among 100 pupils. How many pounds does each pupil receive?' is rated D1 but the answer is 0.075, which requires understanding thousandths and handling a decimal divided by 100. Per the GL research, D1 questions should be 'single-skill with no cross-topic integration' using 'tenths only, or familiar hundredths'. Getting 0.075 from 7.5÷100 involves understanding 3 decimal places.
issues.suggestedFix: Reclassify as D2. The question involves dividing a decimal by 100 and interpreting a thousandths answer, which fits the D2 criteria of 'crossing a place value boundary'.

issues.title: Distractor spacing too tight — rewards guessing over understanding
issues.description: Q49: 'A rectangular label is 0.7 metres wide and 0.6 metres tall. What is its area?' Options are 0.40, 0.42, 0.44, 0.46, 4.2. The first four options are spaced 0.02 apart. A child who understands the method (0.7 × 0.6 = 0.42) will get it right, but the tight spacing means a child who makes a small arithmetic error could land on any of the nearby options. The tight spacing doesn't exploit distinct misconceptions — it just makes the question a precision test.
issues.suggestedFix: Widen the distractor spread to test misconceptions: e.g. 0.42 (correct), 0.13 (added instead of multiplied... 0.7-0.6=0.1, or similar), 4.2 (decimal point error), 0.042 (extra decimal place), 1.3 (added 0.7+0.6). Each distractor should catch a different error pattern.

issues.title: D1 rating too easy for dividing by 100 to get 0.153
issues.description: Q53: 'A school collects £15.30 and divides it equally among 100 charity boxes' is rated D1 but produces a 3-decimal-place answer (0.153). Similar to Q29, this requires understanding that dividing by 100 shifts digits 2 places right and handling a number with initial tens digit. Per GL research, D1 should use 'tenths only, or familiar hundredths'.

issues.title: D1 rating questionable for ÷100 producing 0.091
issues.description: Q75: '9.1 litres poured into 100 tiny pots. How many litres go into each pot?' is D1 but the answer 0.091 requires understanding that the zero in the tenths place is a placeholder. This is more aligned with D2 difficulty based on GL research criteria.

issues.title: Question asks for 'largest time' but context implies fastest runner wants smallest time
issues.description: Q40: 'Five runners record their 50-metre sprint times... Which is the largest time?' This is technically correct (asking for the largest number), but in a sprint context, children associate 'best' with fastest (smallest time). Asking for 'largest time' is slightly counterintuitive and could confuse children who think about performance rather than the numerical value.
issues.suggestedFix: Rephrase to 'Which runner had the slowest time?' or 'Which time is the longest?' to align the question intent with the context. Alternatively, keep as-is since GL does test careful reading of what's being asked.

issues.title: D2 may be too low for £1.25 × 8
issues.description: Q85: 'Each bottle of water costs £1.25. How much do 8 bottles cost?' is rated D2. While £1.25 × 8 requires mental multiplication with hundredths, the partitioning method (1×8=8, 0.25×8=2, total=10) is quite accessible. This sits at the D1/D2 boundary. Not a significant issue — just noting it's on the easy side of D2.
issues.suggestedFix: No change needed — D2 is defensible given the hundredths involved.

issues.title: Explanation method for 0.72 ÷ 0.9 is unclear
issues.description: Q102 explanation says '72 ÷ 90 = 0.8. Or: 7.2 ÷ 9 = 0.8.' The first method (72 ÷ 90) produces 0.8 but the division itself is not obviously easier for a child than the original. The second method (multiplying both by 10) is clearer and matches the lesson content about making the divisor a whole number.
issues.suggestedFix: Rewrite explanation to lead with the clearer method: 'Multiply both numbers by 10 to make the divisor whole: 7.2 ÷ 9 = 0.8. Check: 0.8 × 0.9 = 0.72.'

issues.title: Question requires rounding-up-in-context reasoning that isn't purely decimals
issues.description: Q179: 'A factory makes 12,500 items packed into boxes of 1000. How many boxes are needed?' The mathematical answer is 12.5 but the correct answer is listed as 13 (you can't have half a box). While this is a valid GL-style question testing context interpretation, the question involves whole-number division more than decimals. The decimal element is the interpretation of the 0.5 remainder, but the calculation itself is 12500÷1000 which is a whole-number operation.
issues.suggestedFix: Consider whether this question is better placed in a different topic. If kept in decimals, the question is fine but should be acknowledged as primarily testing 'interpretation of remainders in context' rather than decimal arithmetic.

issues.title: Question involves no decimal arithmetic — pure whole-number division
issues.description: Q181: 'A swimming pool holds 4,750 litres of water. The pool is drained at a rate of 100 litres per minute. How many minutes does it take to drain to exactly 250 litres remaining?' The calculation is (4750-250)÷100 = 4500÷100 = 45. This is entirely whole-number arithmetic. There are no decimals in the question, options, or working. It belongs in a different topic (e.g., long division, or a general problem-solving section).
issues.suggestedFix: Move this question to a more appropriate topic or rework it to involve decimal arithmetic (e.g., draining at 12.5 litres per minute).

issues.title: Duplicate option '6' in interact screen multiple-choice
issues.description: In the decimal-place-value-keyfact lesson's interact screen ('What is the value of the 6 in the tenths column of 24.63?'), the options list contains '6' twice: [0.6, 6, 60, 6, 0.06]. Having the same option appear twice is confusing for children and looks like a data error.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace the duplicate '6' with a different distractor, e.g. '0.006' (thousandths confusion) or '600'.

issues.title: ~20 addition questions mapped to sub-concept with no lessons
issues.description: Questions involving decimal addition (Q2, Q12, Q16, Q25, Q28, Q36, Q44, Q52, Q61, Q71, Q76, Q89, Q95, Q98, Q108, Q125, Q132) are mapped to 'master-column-addition-decimals' which has no lessons (lessonTeachSummary shows '[No lesson found]' and learningGoal is empty). This means children taking the focused learning path for these questions receive no pre-teaching before attempting addition-of-decimals questions.
issues.suggestedFix: Create lessons for this sub-concept (see MATHS-DEC-META-003). Until then, consider remapping these questions to 'subtracting-decimals' which covers the column alignment skill, or create a temporary redirect to that lesson.

issues.title: Whole-number questions mapped to decimal sub-concepts
issues.description: Q179 (12500÷1000=13 boxes) and Q181 (4750-250=4500÷100=45 minutes) involve no decimal arithmetic but are in the decimals topic. Their mapping to 'multiply-by-10-100-1000' and 'divide-by-10-100-1000' sub-concepts is tangentially related but the lessons teach decimal digit-shifting, which isn't what these questions test.
issues.suggestedFix: Either rework the questions to involve decimal numbers, or move them to a more appropriate topic.

issues.title: Explanation could better connect decimal and fraction reasoning
issues.description: Q154: 'In a class, 0.35 walk to school and 0.4 travel by car. The remaining 5 children cycle. How many children in the class?' The explanation is clear but could better bridge the connection between 'the remaining fraction is 0.25' and 'if 0.25 of the class = 5, then 1 whole class = 5÷0.25 = 20'. The step '5 ÷ 0.25 = 20' is presented without explaining WHY you divide.
issues.suggestedFix: Add to explanation: '0.25 means 1/4 of the class. If 1/4 = 5, then the whole class = 5 × 4 = 20. Or: 5 ÷ 0.25 = 20 because we need to find how many groups of 0.25 fit into 5.'

issues.title: Heavy repetition of identical question patterns in original 35 questions
issues.description: The original Q1-Q35 set and the V2 Q36-Q135 set both follow extremely similar patterns. For example: Q1/Q15/Q40/Q48/Q67/Q86/Q100/Q113/Q128 are all 'five items measured, find largest/smallest' comparison questions with near-identical structure. Q2/Q12/Q25/Q36/Q44/Q61/Q76/Q89/Q95/Q108/Q125/Q132 are all 'add two 1dp decimals' questions. While repetition aids practice, the degree of pattern repetition means children may learn to recognise the template rather than develop flexible decimal reasoning.
issues.suggestedFix: Diversify question stems for repeated patterns. For comparison questions, vary the format: some as ordering (already done with Q9, Q56, Q79, Q116, Q145), some as 'which is closest to X' (Q4, Q62), some as 'true or false: 0.45 > 0.5'. For addition, vary the number of decimal places between addends to test alignment skill.

issues.title: Recurring decimals only lightly covered
issues.description: GL research identifies recurring decimals (Trap 10) as a specific test area: '1/3 = 0.333... Children may write 0.3 or 0.33 as the exact equivalent.' The question bank has Q202 (7/11 recurring) and Q204 (5/6 recurring) plus Q194 (4/9 recurring) which touch this, but there are no questions specifically testing whether children understand that some fractions produce non-terminating decimals, or questions asking them to identify which fractions are recurring vs terminating.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 2-3 D3 questions: e.g. 'Which of these fractions gives a recurring decimal? 1/4, 1/5, 1/3, 1/8, 1/10' or 'Which is the best decimal approximation for 1/3? 0.3, 0.33, 0.333, 0.3333, They are all equally good'.
